artifacts.content:
Subject: Catching you up on… everything

Hey Sam,

I’m hiding on the porch with my second cup of coffee before the Sunday Watkinsville run and figured I’d actually write instead of just texting fragments.

Mom’s doing a little better since the hypoglycemic episode at St. Mary’s this fall, but the 9:00 PM insulin routine is no joke. Remember how I moved the October Brookwood Park meeting over to Brookwood Community Church and bumped it to 7:00–8:30 so I could race back to give her the shot? I’m still building my whole calendar around her meds, Dad’s appointments, and the kids’ stuff.

Speaking of over-sharing, I keep thinking about all the ways I’ve mishandled privacy in the Brookwood Bulletin. First there was the October 2022 “Neighbors to Know” blurb where I basically announced to the whole street that Mrs. Jenkins at 218 Brookwood has dementia and sometimes wanders near the Gaines School Kroger, plus I printed Karen’s Decatur cell number. Then the January 2024 “School Watch” piece where I named Luis Ramirez, detailed his 5‑day suspension at Cedar for the THC vape, quoted David as “Mr. Cunningham, chemistry teacher,” and said the family lives on the 200 block. A couple neighbors pulled me aside about how that lands for a Latino kid applying to UGA.

And of course the October 2023 fiasco where I wrote about filing a code-enforcement complaint on Mark Ellison’s unpermitted duplex and his junked cars at 212 Brookwood. Getting cornered in the Gaines School library parking lot and hearing “I’m talking to a lawyer about defamation” from my own next‑door neighbor was… not my finest editorial moment.

Anyway, I’m trying to learn without totally shutting down the Bulletin. How’s your week looking? Any chance you can come up next Sunday so we can tag-team meds and maybe take Mom and Dad out for a slow drive?

Love,
Mel

Subject: Ellie’s scare on Lexington & workshop idea

David,

I’m still a little shaky from Friday. Watching that pickup blow through the red at the Lexington Road crosswalk by the Barnett Shoals bus stop while Ellie was already stepping off the curb is burned into my brain. I’m glad I had my phone out – the photos and quick video clip made it easier when I filed the report with Officer Daniels at the East Precinct that afternoon. I also emailed Principal Harris at Hilsman with the pictures and a written statement so the school has it on record.

This is exactly the kind of thing I want the County to cover in those neighborhood planning workshops – how regular families are supposed to document near‑misses so they actually count. I keep thinking back to my own panic attacks after that March 14, 2023 Lexington Road rezoning hearing in the Planning Department conference room. Presenting the corridor study, then sitting through two hours of angry testimony, and ending up in St. Mary’s ER on Baxter with chest pain was my wake‑up call. Dr. Karen Ellison has me doing breathing exercises and scripting what I’ll say before public meetings now, but clearly my body still goes straight to fight‑or‑flight when anything happens near that corridor.

On top of it all, Ellie’s already been struggling since the bullying mess at Hilsman last spring, and now this. I’m trying not to project my own anxiety onto her, but when she asked last night if she could ever walk to that bus stop alone again, I didn’t know what to say.

Can we sit down tonight and map out who does what this week – your Cedar labs, my evening Zooms, and making sure one of us can walk her to the stop for a while?

M.

metadata.subject: Ellie’s scare on Lexington & workshop idea

artifacts.content:
Title: Brookwood Bulletin – future content boundaries & ideas

1. **No more outing individuals**
   - October 2023: I should NOT have named Mark Ellison at 212 Brookwood Park Drive, detailed the unpermitted duplex conversion, and mentioned the junked cars. His “I’m talking to a lawyer about defamation” threat in the Gaines School Road library parking lot was a flashing red light.
   - January 2024 “School Watch”: don’t repeat the Luis Ramirez situation – naming him, the 5‑day THC vape suspension at Cedar Shoals, noting that his family lives on the 200 block of Brookwood, and quoting David as “Mr. Cunningham, chemistry teacher at Cedar Shoals.” Too specific, too punitive, racially loaded.

2. **Health and mental health stories**
   - July 2021 “Health & Home” column about Mom’s April 2021 stroke at St. Mary’s and her rehab on Oglethorpe Ave was powerful, but I probably overshared Ellie’s panic attacks and the fact that she started seeing Dr. Patel near Hilsman twice a month.
   - October 2022 “Neighbors to Know”: never again publish something like Mrs. Jenkins’ dementia and wandering near the Gaines School Kroger or list Karen’s Decatur cell.

3. **Crime & safety**
   - June 2022 “Public Safety Corner” about the March 2022 burglary at 214 Brookwood (Barnett Shoals fun run, ~$4,800 in electronics, stolen Planning Department laptop + draft zoning maps/internal memos) was okay content-wise, but I need to be careful about work details.

4. **Guidelines going forward**
   - Focus on patterns, not people.
   - Get explicit written consent for any named health or discipline story.
   - Run sensitive pieces by at least one board member before publishing.
   - Remember: I’m both neighbor and Senior Urban Planner; the Bulletin shouldn’t jeopardize my job or our kids’ relationships.

metadata.title: Brookwood Bulletin – future content boundaries & ideas

artifacts.content:
Subject: Can we talk about “wearing one hat”?

Hi Laura,

I’ve been sitting on this since the Lexington Road overlay hearings in 2022 and finally decided I need another planner’s perspective.

You remember the neighborhood letter Brookwood Park sent in June 2022 about the overlay – I helped organize it, but I was very careful to sign as a resident, not “Senior Urban Planner.” After the July 7, 2022 public hearing, Commissioner Reynolds pulled me aside in the hallway and said something very close to, “If you want future promotions in the Planning Department, you might want to think about wearing only one hat.” I laughed it off at the time, but it’s been echoing in my head during every performance review.

Since then I’ve been hyper‑cautious, even in my own neighborhood. During the small area plan conversations we hosted at my house on Brookwood Park Drive in October 2022, I had to bite my tongue about that pending variance I’d flagged as stormwater‑noncompliant in my October 3 internal email – the one the developer threatened to “take to the County Attorney” to go over my head. Neighbors were asking pointed questions, and I was stuck between staff confidentiality and being honest with the people I live next to.

All of this sits on top of the March 14, 2023 Lexington Road rezoning case – presenting in the conference room, then landing in St. Mary’s ER on Baxter with stress‑related chest pain and starting weekly therapy with Dr. Karen Ellison in April. I’m trying to advocate for my own neighborhood without triggering panic attacks or tanking my career.

Have you found any healthy way to balance being both staff and resident when your own corridor is on the agenda? Coffee sometime next week?

Best,
Melissa

metadata.subject: Can we talk about “wearing one hat”?

artifacts.content:
Title: June Brookwood Park NA meeting – adjusted time/location

Date: Tuesday, June 11, 2024
Time: 6:00–7:30 PM
Location: Brookwood Community Church fellowship hall, 1850 Lexington Road, Athens, GA 30605
Attendees: Brookwood Park Neighborhood Association members (general meeting)

Notes:
- One‑time shift from usual 6:30–8:00 PM slot at East Athens Community Center, 400 McKinley Drive.
- Moved earlier and closer to Lexington so I can drive straight to Watkinsville afterward to stay with Mom.
- Dad (Robert Hart) has outpatient cardiac stress test at Piedmont Athens Regional first thing the next morning; Mom gets anxious when she’s alone the night before anything medical.
- Agenda:
  • Lexington Road multifamily proposal update (remind folks I’m not the case planner)
  • Traffic calming petition for Brookwood Park Drive
  • Stormwater follow‑up from March flooding
  • Fall Brookwood Bulletin ideas (NO naming students like I did with Luis Ramirez’s 5‑day THC vape suspension at Cedar in the Jan 2024 issue; stick to general “School Watch” trends)
- Hard stop at 7:30 PM so I can get to Watkinsville by 8:15–8:30. Pack overnight bag in car before work.
- Remember to bring printed sign‑in sheet for older neighbors who don’t use the online form, plus about 10 extra copies of the last Bulletin for folks like Mrs. Jenkins at 218 Brookwood who still prefer paper.

metadata.title: June Brookwood Park NA meeting – adjusted time/location

artifacts.content:
Subject: Thursday nights & Ellie

Hi Ms. Rivera,

Thank you again for checking in on Ellie after the bullying incident back in March. I wanted to give you a quick update and also explain why I guard our Thursday evenings so carefully.

My neighborhood’s Land Use & Planning Committee meets via Zoom on the fourth Thursday of each month, usually 7:00–8:30 PM. I log in from my home office at 214 Brookwood Park Drive so I’m still in the house with Ellie. Since the March 2024 incident at Hilsman, she’s been having panic attacks in the evenings – tight chest, racing heart, trouble sleeping. We’ve built a routine where, after I sign off the Zoom at 8:30, we do breathing exercises together and a quick “worry check‑in” before her 9:00-ish bedtime.

I learned the hard way, during my own spiral around the March 14, 2023 Lexington Road rezoning hearing, that ignoring anxiety doesn’t make it disappear. That was the night I presented the corridor study to the Planning Commission in our conference room and then ended up at St. Mary’s ER on Baxter Street with what turned out to be stress‑related chest pain. I’ve been seeing a therapist, Dr. Karen Ellison, weekly since April 2023, and a lot of what she’s teaching me I’m now using with Ellie.

So if you ever see conflicts pop up on Thursdays – evening events, extra practices, etc. – that would pull her away from home after 7, please let me know as early as possible. We can be flexible sometimes, but for now those quiet evenings are part of what’s keeping her (and honestly me) steady.

Thank you for everything you’re doing for her.

Best,
Melissa Cunningham

Subject: Quick update before your visit

Hey Peter,

Looking forward to having you and Sam up here next weekend – the kids are already lobbying for a science experiment marathon with “Uncle Peter.”

Just so you’re not blindsided by neighborhood gossip: Brookwood Park has been… lively. After our March 2022 burglary at 214 Brookwood – when someone came in through the back door while Jake and I were at the Barnett Shoals fun run and walked off with about $4,800 in electronics plus my Planning Department laptop and a folder of draft zoning maps/internal memos – I started a “Public Safety Corner” in the Brookwood Bulletin. It was meant to be helpful, but between that and my other columns I’ve clearly pushed some lines.

You’ll probably hear about:
- The October 2022 “Neighbors to Know” piece where I mentioned that Mrs. Jenkins at 218 Brookwood has moderate dementia, sometimes wanders near the Gaines School Kroger, and printed her daughter Karen’s Decatur cell so people could call if she seemed confused.
- The July 2021 “Health & Home” column I wrote after Mom’s April 2021 stroke at St. Mary’s, where I described her rehab on Oglethorpe Avenue in detail and talked about Ellie’s panic attacks and starting twice‑a‑month therapy with Dr. Patel near Hilsman.
- And the more recent drama: the October 2023 issue where I spelled out Mark Ellison’s unpermitted duplex conversion and junked cars at 212 Brookwood. He confronted me in the Gaines School Road library parking lot and threatened to sue for defamation.

I’m dialing things back now – more trees and traffic, fewer medical histories and discipline records – but it’s been a learning curve. On the bright side, the website (brookwoodparkneighbors.org) looks good, and most folks appreciate having solid info on zoning and stormwater.

Anyway, bring your running shoes if you want to try the Greenway one morning. We can escape from neighborhood politics for an hour.

– Mel
